ati-micro.conclusion: Acute tubular injury with tubular epithelial cell cytoplasmic microvacuolation
ati-micro.comments: There is acute tubular injury with isometric clear vacuolation of tubular epithelial cells. Possible causes include acute tubular toxicity related to calcineurin inhibitors (tends to be focal), or osmotic tubulopathy (tends to be diffuse). The latter can be seen with plasma expanders (e.g. mannitol, dextran), some pre-transplant perfusion solutions (containing hydroxyethyl starch), some radiological contrast solutions, IVIg (due to the stabilising agents) and SGLT2i (due to inhibition of glucose resorption in proximal S1 tubule).

diab.main_body: Glomeruli are abnormal and show a marked, often nodular increase in mesangial matrix, with a mild increase in mesangial cellularity. Capillary walls are also markedly thickened.
diab.comments: Glomerular basement membranes are thickened and there is an increase in mesangial matrix. Although not specific, the findings are most often associated with diabetes – although the patient is not stated to be diabetic, please correlate with glycaemic parameters. In the absence of diabetes, such features are referred to as “idiopathic nodular glomerulosclerosis” and can be seen in association with smoking and hypertension (Idiopathic Nodular Glomerulosclerosis Is a Distinct Clinicopathologic Entity Linked to Hypertension and Smoking.  Markowitz G et al. Human Pathology 2002)

fsgs.comments: In the post-transplant setting, glomerular segmental sclerosis can be recurrence of primary FSGS or, more commonly, a glomerular pattern developed secondary to a number of glomerular insults. Careful clinico-pathological correlation is needed to distinguish these. A history of (or evocative of) primary FSGS in the development of ESRF, early post-transplant development of new onset proteinuria, and extensive foot process effacement of epithelial cells would favour recurrent primary FSGS.Late post-transplant development of new onset proteinuria, previous or concurrent significant glomerular or tubulointerstitial disease of a different nature (e.g. alloimmune), as well as segmental foot process effacement favour secondary adaptive FSGS.  This can be seen in association with reduction of functioning nephrons, increased haemodynamic stress on a normal nephron population, or in association with some infections (e.g. HIV) and drugs (e.g. CNI, heroin). In this case, the features overall favour...

igm.conclusion: Features in keeping with IgM nephropathy

lccn.comments: in routine clinical practice, we propose to report only the highest number of casts per square millimeter in the cortex, using simple cutoffs of <5, 5 to 10, and >10 casts/mm2. https://doi.org/10.1182/blood.2019003807

neut.comments: In view of the small focus of neutrophilic tubulitis, please exclude a urinary tract infection.

t3.main_body: Severe tubulitis is present in >1 focus of non-severely atrophic tubules (t3).
